examples.title: Algorithm: NETCONF-YANG IoT Device Management Workflow
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the five-step workflow for managing IoT devices using NETCONF-YANG framework
examples.code:
ALGORITHM NETCONF_IoT_Management
INPUT: List of IoT devices, YANG model, configuration parameters
OUTPUT: Configured and monitored IoT devices

BEGIN
    # Step 1: Device Discovery and Capability Exchange
    FUNCTION Device_Discovery(devices):
        FOR each device IN devices DO:
            Establish_SSH_Connection(device)
            Send_Hello_Message()
            Receive_Hello_Message(device)
            capabilities = Parse_Capabilities(device.hello)
            device.supported_models = Filter_YANG_Models(capabilities)
            IF NOT Has_Required_Model(device) THEN:
                Log_Warning("Device " + device.id + " lacks required YANG model")
            END IF
        END FOR
        RETURN devices_with_capabilities
    END FUNCTION

    # Step 2: Configuration Deployment using Candidate Datastore
    FUNCTION Deploy_Configuration(device, config_data):
        # Lock the candidate datastore
        Send_RPC(device, "<lock><target><candidate/></target></lock>")
        
        # Clear and prepare candidate configuration
        Send_RPC(device, "<edit-config><target><candidate/></target><config>")
        
        # Apply YANG-structured configuration
        FOR each config_item IN config_data DO:
            xml_config = Transform_to_XML(config_item, YANG_Model)
            Send_RPC(device, xml_config)
        END FOR
        
        # Validate configuration (test option)
        Send_RPC(device, "<validate><source><candidate/></source></validate>")
        
        # Commit to running configuration
        Send_RPC(device, "<commit/>")
        
        # Unlock the datastore
        Send_RPC(device, "<unlock><target><candidate/></target></unlock>")
        
        RETURN success_status
    END FUNCTION

    # Step 3: Monitoring - Retrieve Operational State
    FUNCTION Monitor_Devices(devices):
        FOR each device IN devices DO:
            # Get both configuration and operational state
            response = Send_RPC(device, "<get><filter type='subtree'>" +
                "<sensor-state xmlns='http://example.com/iot-sensor'/>" +
                "</filter></get>")
            
            state_data = Parse_XML_Response(response)
            device.temperature = state_data.temperature
            device.battery_level = state_data.battery_level
            device.last_update = state_data.timestamp
            
            # Check for anomalies
            IF device.temperature > device.threshold THEN
                Trigger_Alert("High temperature: " + device.temperature)
            END IF
        END FOR
        RETURN device_states
    END FUNCTION

    # Step 4: Automated Response based on Monitoring
    FUNCTION Automated_Response(device_states):
        FOR each device IN device_states DO:
            IF device.battery_level < 15 THEN
                # Switch to low-power mode
                new_config = Create_Config(sampling_interval=300, low_power=true)
                Deploy_Configuration(device, new_config)
                Log_Action("Device " + device.id + " switched to low-power mode")
            END IF
            
            IF device.temperature > device.threshold THEN
                # Increase sampling frequency
                new_config = Create_Config(sampling_interval=10)
                Deploy_Configuration(device, new_config)
                Log_Action("Device " + device.id + " increased sampling frequency")
            END IF
        END FOR
    END FUNCTION

    # Step 5: Maintenance with Rollback Support
    FUNCTION Maintenance_With_Rollback(devices, firmware_version):
        FOR each device IN devices DO:
            # Create configuration backup
            backup = Send_RPC(device, "<get-config><source><running/></source></get-config>")
            Store_Backup(device.id, backup)
            
            TRY:
                # Apply maintenance (firmware update or config change)
                Apply_Maintenance(device, firmware_version)
                
                # Verify device is operational
                IF NOT Verify_Device_Health(device) THEN
                    RAISE error
                END IF
                
            EXCEPT:
                # Automatic rollback on failure
                Rollback_Configuration(device.id, backup)
                Log_Error("Maintenance failed for " + device.id + ", rolled back")
            END TRY
        END FOR
    END FUNCTION

    # Main execution
    FUNCTION Main():
        # Initialize
        devices = Discover_Available_Devices()
        authenticated_devices = Device_Discovery(devices)
        
        # Deploy initial configuration
        base_config = Load_YANG_Configuration()
        FOR each device IN authenticated_devices DO
            Deploy_Configuration(device, base_config)
        END FOR
        
        # Continuous monitoring loop
        WHILE system_active DO
            states = Monitor_Devices(authenticated_devices)
            Automated_Response(states)
            Wait(sampling_interval)
        END WHILE
    END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the complete NETCONF-YANG IoT management workflow: (1) Device Discovery - establishing NETCONF sessions via SSH and exchanging YANG capability hello messages, (2) Configuration Deployment - using the candidate datastore with lock/commit/unlock operations for atomic changes, (3) Monitoring - retrieving operational state using get operations with XML filters, (4) Automated Response - making configuration changes based on device state, and (5) Maintenance - with rollback capability using configuration backups.

examples.title: Python: NETCONF Client for IoT Device Management
examples.description: Python implementation showing how to connect to IoT devices using NETCONF, exchange capabilities, and perform configuration operations
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
NETCONF Client for IoT Device Management
Demonstrates connection, capability exchange, and configuration operations
"""

import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Dict, Optional
import socket
import select
import time

# Constants for NETCONF namespaces
NETCONF_NS = "urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0"
IOT_SENSOR_NS = "http://example.com/iot-sensor"

# XML Message Templates
HELLO_TEMPLATE = '''<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<hello xmlns="{ns}">
  <capabilities>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:base:1.1</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:candidate:1.0</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:validated:1.0</capability>
    <capability>urn:ietf:params:netconf:capability:rollback-on-error:1.0</capability>
    <capability>{model_ns}</capability>
  </capabilities>
</hello>'''

RPC_TEMPLATE = '''<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<rpc message-id="{msg_id}" xmlns="{ns}">
  {operation}
</rpc>'''

@dataclass
class IoTDevice:
    """Represents an IoT device with NETCONF support"""
    host: str
    port: int = 830  # NETCONF over SSH default port
    device_id: str = ""
    capabilities: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    temperature: float = 0.0
    battery_level: int = 100
    sampling_interval: int = 60
    is_connected: bool = False


class NETCONFClient:
    """NETCONF client for managing IoT devices"""
    
    def __init__(self, host: str, port: int = 830):
        self.host = host
        self.port = port
        self.socket = None
        self.session_id = None
        self.message_id = 1
        self.capabilities = []
    
    def connect(self, username: str, password: str) -> bool:
        """Establish SSH connection and perform NETCONF hello exchange"""
        try:
            # In production, use paramiko or ncclient for actual SSH
            print(f"[NETCONF] Connecting to {self.host}:{self.port}...")
            
            # Simulate SSH connection (in real implementation, use paramiko)
            # self.socket = paramiko.SSHClient()
            # self.socket.connect(self.host, port=self.port, username=username, password=password)
            # transport = self.socket.get_transport()
            # channel = transport.open_session()
            # channel.exec_command("netconf")
            
            print("[NETCONF] SSH connection established")
            
            # Send client hello
            hello_msg = HELLO_TEMPLATE.format(ns=NETCONF_NS, model_ns=IOT_SENSOR_NS)
            self._send_message(hello_msg)
            
            # Receive server hello
            server_hello = self._receive_message()
            self._parse_hello(server_hello)
            
            self.capabilities = self._extract_capabilities(server_hello)
            print(f"[NETCONF] Server capabilities: {len(self.capabilities)} features")
            print(f"[NETCONF] Session established with ID: {self.session_id}")
            
            return True
            
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[NETCONF] Connection failed: {e}")
            return False
    
    def _send_message(self, message: str):
        """Send NETCONF message with proper framing"""
        # NETCONF uses special framing: length + message
        # In real implementation: frame = f"#{len(message)}#\n{message}"
        print(f"[NETCONF] Sending: {message[:100]}...")
        pass
    
    def _receive_message(self) -> str:
        """Receive NETCONF message with proper framing"""
        # In real implementation: read framed message
        return ""  # Placeholder
    
    def _parse_hello(self, hello_xml: str):
        """Parse server hello message to extract session ID and capabilities"""
        try:
            root = ET.fromstring(hello_xml)
            ns = {"nc": NETCONF_NS}
            
            # Extract session ID
            session_elem = root.find("nc:session-id", ns)
            if session_elem is not None:
                self.session_id = session_elem.text
            
            # Extract capabilities
            for cap in root.findall(".//nc:capability", ns):
                self.capabilities.append(cap.text)
                
        except ET.ParseError as e:
            print(f"[NETCONF] Hello parse error: {e}")
    
    def _extract_capabilities(self, hello_xml: str) -> List[str]:
        """Extract and return list of capabilities"""
        return self.capabilities
    
    def get_config(self, source: str = "running", filter_xml: str = "") -> Dict:
        """Retrieve configuration from specified datastore"""
        filter_part = f"<filter type='subtree'>{filter_xml}</filter>" if filter_xml else ""
        
        operation = f"""
        <get-config>
            <source><{source}/></source>
            {filter_part}
        </get-config>"""
        
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        # Parse and return configuration
        return self._parse_config_response(response)
    
    def edit_config(self, config_xml: str, target: str = "candidate") -> bool:
        """Modify configuration in the target datastore"""
        operation = f"""
        <edit-config>
            <target><{target}/></target>
            <config>
                {config_xml}
            </config>
            <error-option>rollback-on-error</error-option>
        </edit-config>"""
        
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        return self._check_ok_response(response)
    
    def commit(self) -> bool:
        """Commit candidate configuration to running"""
        operation = "<commit/>"
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        return self._check_ok_response(response)
    
    def lock(self, target: str = "candidate") -> bool:
        """Lock a datastore to prevent concurrent modifications"""
        operation = f"<lock><target><{target}/></target></lock>"
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        return self._check_ok_response(response)
    
    def unlock(self, target: str = "candidate") -> bool:
        """Unlock a previously locked datastore"""
        operation = f"<unlock><target><{target}/></target></unlock>"
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        return self._check_ok_response(response)
    
    def get(self, filter_xml: str = "") -> Dict:
        """Retrieve both configuration and operational state"""
        filter_part = f"<filter type='subtree'>{filter_xml}</filter>" if filter_xml else ""
        
        operation = f"<get>{filter_part}</get>"
        
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        response = self._receive_message()
        
        return self._parse_state_data(response)
    
    def _check_ok_response(self, response: str) -> bool:
        """Check if NETCONF response contains <ok/>"""
        try:
            root = ET.fromstring(response)
            ns = {"nc": NETCONF_NS}
            ok_elem = root.find("nc:ok", ns)
            return ok_elem is not None
        except ET.ParseError:
            return False
    
    def _parse_config_response(self, response: str) -> Dict:
        """Parse configuration data from NETCONF response"""
        # Implementation would parse XML configuration
        return {"data": "parsed configuration"}
    
    def _parse_state_data(self, response: str) -> Dict:
        """Parse operational state data from NETCONF response"""
        # Implementation would parse XML state data
        return {"state": "parsed state data"}
    
    def close_session(self):
        """Gracefully close NETCONF session"""
        operation = "<close-session/>"
        rpc_msg = RPC_TEMPLATE.format(
            msg_id=str(self.message_id),
            ns=NETCONF_NS,
            operation=operation
        )
        self.message_id += 1
        
        self._send_message(rpc_msg)
        print("[NETCONF] Session closed")


def configure_iot_sensor(client: NETCONFClient, device_id: str, 
                         sampling_interval: int, threshold: float) -> bool:
    """
    Configure an IoT temperature sensor using NETCONF edit-config
    """
    # Build YANG-structured configuration XML
    config_xml = f"""
    <sensor-config xmlns="{IOT_SENSOR_NS}">
        <device-id>{device_id}</device-id>
        <sampling-interval>{sampling_interval}</sampling-interval>
        <reporting-threshold>{threshold}</reporting-threshold>
    </sensor-config>"""
    
    # Lock candidate datastore
    if not client.lock("candidate"):
        print("[ERROR] Failed to lock candidate datastore")
        return False
    
    # Edit configuration
    if not client.edit_config(config_xml, "candidate"):
        print("[ERROR] Failed to edit configuration")
        client.unlock("candidate")
        return False
    
    # Commit to running
    if not client.commit():
        print("[ERROR] Failed to commit configuration")
        client.unlock("candidate")
        return False
    
    # Unlock datastore
    client.unlock("candidate")
    
    print(f"[SUCCESS] Device {device_id} configured successfully")
    return True


def monitor_iot_device(client: NETCONFClient) -> Dict:
    """
    Monitor IoT device operational state using NETCONF get
    """
    # Build filter for sensor state data
    filter_xml = f"""
    <sensor-state xmlns="{IOT_SENSOR_NS}">
        <temperature/>
        <battery-level/>
    </sensor-state>"""
    
    # Get operational state
    state = client.get(filter_xml)
    
    return state


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create NETCONF client for IoT device
    client = NETCONFClient(host="192.168.1.100", port=830)
    
    # Connect to device (in real usage, provide credentials)
    if client.connect("admin", "password"):
        # Configure sensor device
        configure_iot_sensor(
            client=client,
            device_id="temp-sensor-042",
            sampling_interval=30,
            threshold=25.0
        )
        
        # Monitor device state
        state = monitor_iot_device(client)
        print(f"Device state: {state}")
        
        # Close session
        client.close_session()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates a NETCONF client for IoT device management with key features: (1) SSH connection establishment with NETCONF hello exchange, (2) Capability negotiation showing YANG model support, (3) Lock/commit/unlock operations for atomic configuration changes, (4) get-config and edit-config operations for configuration management, (5) get operation for retrieving operational state with XML filters, (6) Error handling with rollback-on-error option, and (7) YANG-structured XML configuration matching the sensor model from the topic.

examples.title: C: YANG Data Structures and NETCONF Message Handling
examples.description: C implementation showing YANG model data structures, NETCONF RPC message generation, and datastore operations for IoT device management
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates YANG data structures and NETCONF message handling for IoT device management: (1) YANG data structure definitions mirroring the topic's sensor model with leaf, container constructs, (2) YangLeafUint32 and YangLeafDecimal64 types matching YANG data types with defaults, (3) NETCONF RPC message generation functions for hello, get-config, edit-config, get, lock, unlock, commit operations, (4) YANG configuration XML builder creating properly namespaced sensor-config elements, (5) Transaction workflow with lock-edit-commit-unlock pattern for atomic configuration changes, and (6) Operational state filter for reading sensor-state (config=false) data. The implementation follows the four-layer NETCONF architecture from the topic.
